author: @bearnaff9387
text: Aren't Borromean Hairpins good for any situation where, when one displaces two axes against one another so that they move apart, a third axis is also displaced?  This seems like something worth exploring for expanding and unfolding structural mechanisms, especially ones where a certain degree of stiffness at the joint is needed.

author: @tomgeorge3726
text:
A nice collection of gear trains Robert.
The set of Nautilus Gears, at 4:26 , has been used in a number of machines.
If you get the output shaft to push a lever that raises and lowers a table, you can get a slow rise time with lots of torque and  a fast lowering time using the tables mass for it to drop.
Also two circular gears with offset axels will give you a rising and lowering continuous output speed/torque.
I worked in a brick factory and they used this principle to raise a table of bricks to another level to be pushed off, then lowered the table, empty to get another load slid on.
Low speed , high torque UP, high speed low torque down.

Thank you again for your amazing videos. I really appreciate your hard work researching, recording, editing and posting!

replies.author: @bertjesklotepino
replies.text:
There is a machine that does absolutely nothing but just turn gears.
I can't remember what it is called.
BUT it is a pretty large piece of equipment that has all kinds of gears, levers, for to turn other gears and levers, and that kinda stuff.
And not to show time, but just to turn the gears.
A nonsense machine basically.

I have seen a video of it a long time ago.
It was just a "machine" to show off all kinds of neat connections, etc.

I will look into my archive a bit to find out the name of it.
But just imagine a huge marble machine kinda thing. A clock, but no hands to keep time.
Just a bunch of cogs and gears and escapements and what have you, all put together, to do absolute nothing but just turn around.
A showcase, basically.

I am almost certain that mr ThinkingandTinkering knows the "machine" i am talking about.
And if not, i will have to dive deeper and a bit faster to retrieve the name and title of the video.

author: @jamesross1003
text: The thing that blows my mind is that everything(and I mean everything) is made of gears.  Atoms are gears.  Think about that for a moment.  Solar systems are gears.  Galaxies are gears.  The driving mechanism behind each may differ, but they are gears.  Even an electrical system is a set of gears.  Current flow through the medium(wires ect.) is a ratchet type to planetary gear depending on the point of perspective.  Thanks Rob!

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I have LOL!  Thanks again Rob!

author: @JungleJargon
text: I made a hydraulic transmission sort of like a variable speed transmission. The speed of light C isn’t constant since the measures of time and distance aren’t constant over larger distances. Changing from 60 kilometers an hour to 60 miles an hour increases your speed. Then if you go from 60 miles an hour to 60 miles a minute it increases your speed again. This what is happening in general relativity when less gravity increases the measure of distance and speeds up the rate of time over large distances. It also means that the earth is younger than what they claim. Think about it as a mechanic.

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Way back in  the 70's 3M company had a product call Fibertran, it was something similar to "bear hair" they used it for one-way clutches. The surfaces were covered with short bristles that were laid over they would slip in one direction and engage in the other direction.
